prompt: School bus pre-trip inspection should include checking:

options.text: Lights, mirrors, emergency exits, tires, brakes, and required safety equipment
Only the radio station presets
Whether the bus color matches the school mascot exactly
Skipping brake checks if running late

explanation: A thorough pre-trip verifies systems that protect students and road users before departure.

distractorExplanations.b: Entertainment settings are not safety-critical checks.
distractorExplanations.c: Paint aesthetics are not the inspection focus.

prompt: Convex (fisheye) mirrors on a school bus are used primarily to:

options.text: Replace the need to look for students at all
Show wide areas along the sides and front danger zones that flat mirrors may miss
Heat the windshield
Measure vehicle speed

explanation: Convex mirrors expand field of view for loading-zone and side blind spots; drivers still must scan properly.

distractorExplanations.c: Defrosters heat glass, not mirrors' purpose.

prompt: The danger zone around a school bus is generally considered:

options.text: Only the inside aisle
Five miles ahead on the highway
The area extending about 10 feet around the bus where students are at greatest risk
The school parking lot exclusively when empty

explanation: Most student injuries occur near the bus; drivers must account for the danger zone in mirror use and procedures.

distractorExplanations.a: Interior supervision matters, but danger zone refers to exterior risk areas.
distractorExplanations.b: Highway distance is not the danger-zone definition.
distractorExplanations.d: Danger zone exists whenever students are around the bus.

prompt: If an emergency exit warning buzzer sounds because a door/window is open while driving, you should:

options.text: Disable the buzzer and keep driving with the exit open
Accelerate to drown out the sound
Ignore it if students say it is fine
Stop safely and secure/close the exit properly before continuing

explanation: Open emergency exits while underway are unsafe; stop and correct the condition.

distractorExplanations.a: Disabling warnings hides a safety defect.
distractorExplanations.c: Student opinion does not override equipment rules.

prompt: School bus crossing gates (stop arms) and red lights are used to:

options.text: Stop other traffic while students board or leave the bus as required by law
Decorate the bus for parades only
Signal that the driver is on a personal errand
Replace mirrors entirely

explanation: Eight-light warning systems and stop arms control traffic during student loading/unloading.

distractorExplanations.b: They are operational safety devices, not decorations.

prompt: During pre-trip, tire checks should verify:

options.text: Only that tires are black
Proper inflation, tread, and no dangerous cuts/bulges or duals touching
That spare tire logos match
Nothing—tires never fail on buses

explanation: Tire defects cause blowouts and loss of control; duals rubbing indicate problems.

distractorExplanations.c: Logo matching is not a safety check.
distractorExplanations.d: Tires do fail; inspection prevents many failures.

prompt: ABS malfunction lamps on a school bus mean:

options.text: The bus has no brakes at all always
You must pump brakes rapidly at all times
ABS may not function; drive carefully and get repairs—use normal braking without pumping
You should remove the fuse and ignore it forever

explanation: ABS faults do not eliminate foundation brakes; do not pump ABS-equipped brakes in panic stops.

prompt: First-aid kits and body fluid cleanup kits on school buses should be:

options.text: Optional decorations
Locked in a garage miles away during routes
Used as student toys
Present, accessible, and stocked per state/district requirements

explanation: Emergency equipment must be on the bus and ready for injuries/cleanup incidents.

distractorExplanations.b: It must be on the vehicle in service.
distractorExplanations.c: Kits are for emergencies, not play.

prompt: Fire extinguishers on school buses must be:

options.text: Charged, properly mounted, and accessible to the driver
Empty to save weight
Hidden under loose trash
Removed to make room for sports gear

explanation: A usable extinguisher is part of required emergency equipment.

distractorExplanations.b: Empty units are useless in a fire.

prompt: If windshield wipers fail in heavy rain during a route, you should:

options.text: Speed up to finish faster
Stop in a safe place and not continue until visibility is safe/repairs are made per policy
Drive blind using memory of the road
Have a student wipe the glass from outside while moving

explanation: Inadequate visibility is a stop condition; student involvement outside a moving bus is dangerous.

distractorExplanations.d: Students must not wipe a moving bus.

prompt: Seat belt (if equipped for driver) use by the school bus driver is:

options.text: Optional because buses never crash
Only for highway trips over 100 miles
Required/expected—drivers must use the driver restraint as designed
Prohibited by federal law always

explanation: Driver restraints protect the person responsible for student safety; use them.

distractorExplanations.b: Use is not limited to long trips.

prompt: Emergency roof hatches should be:

options.text: Painted shut permanently
Blocked with luggage
Ignored because side doors always suffice in every scenario
Operational, unobstructed, and included in pre-trip checks

explanation: Multiple egress paths are required for evacuation options; hatches must work.

distractorExplanations.c: Alternate exits are needed if primary exits are blocked.

prompt: Student-facing cameras/GPS (if installed) are primarily for:

options.text: Safety, security, and operational accountability per district policy
Entertainment livestreams for the public without authorization
Replacing the driver's duty to supervise
Increasing road speed limits for the bus

explanation: Technology supports—but does not replace—safe driving and supervision practices.

distractorExplanations.b: Unauthorized public streams may violate privacy/policy.
distractorExplanations.d: Speed limits are set by law, not cameras.

prompt: If you find a defective stop-arm during pre-trip, you should:

options.text: Cover it with tape and run the route anyway
Report it and not operate until repaired/cleared per district and law
Tell students to flag traffic themselves
Use hazard lights only as a permanent substitute without authorization

explanation: Inoperative student-loading warning equipment is a serious out-of-service type defect.

prompt: Air brake adjustment (if air-equipped) matters because:

options.text: Loose adjustment improves fuel economy only
Brakes work better when severely out of adjustment
Excessive pushrod travel reduces braking effectiveness
Adjustment is unrelated to stopping distance

explanation: Out-of-adjustment brakes increase stopping distance and fail inspections.

distractorExplanations.a: Adjustment is a safety, not MPG, issue.

prompt: When approaching a stop to load students, you should:

options.text: Approach carefully, activate amber warning lights as required, and scan for traffic/students
Race to the stop and brake hard at the last second
Never use warning lights
Stop in the middle of an uncontrolled blind curve without adjusting

explanation: Smooth, legal warning-light procedures and scanning protect students in the danger zone.

distractorExplanations.b: Hard stops endanger students and traffic.
distractorExplanations.c: Warning lights are required in the process.
distractorExplanations.d: Stops need adequate sight distance when possible.

prompt: Students should be taught to cross in front of the bus only after:

options.text: They run behind the bus out of mirror view
The driver signals it is safe and they have checked traffic themselves
They cross on red without looking
They retrieve dropped papers from under the bus while it may move

explanation: Crossing procedures keep students in view; never go behind or under the bus.

distractorExplanations.a: Rear crossing is a major fatality pattern.
distractorExplanations.c: Looking and waiting for the signal are essential.
distractorExplanations.d: Items under the bus require driver help—not student retrieval while at risk.

prompt: If a student drops papers near the bus during unloading, the student should:

options.text: Dive under the bus immediately
Chase papers into traffic
Tell the driver and wait for instructions—never go under/near wheels to grab items
Assume the driver sees them without communication

explanation: Communication prevents students from entering blind spots near wheels.

prompt: Railroad crossing procedures for school buses generally require:

options.text: Crossing tracks without slowing if the bus is late
Relying only on students to watch for trains
Stopping on the tracks to count riders
Stopping, looking and listening, and crossing only when safe (with required hazards/procedures)

explanation: School buses must follow railroad stop requirements; never stop on tracks.

distractorExplanations.a: Lateness does not waive RR stops.
distractorExplanations.b: The driver is responsible for the scan.
distractorExplanations.c: Stopping on tracks risks being struck.

prompt: At a railroad crossing, if your bus stalls on the tracks, you should:

options.text: Evacuate students immediately away from the tracks at an angle toward the train's approach if a train is coming
Keep students seated to wait for a tow
Try to push the bus with students still aboard while a train approaches
Honk continuously without evacuating when a train is imminent

explanation: Life safety comes first—evacuate clear of tracks when stalled and danger exists.

distractorExplanations.b: Waiting aboard on tracks is deadly if a train comes.
distractorExplanations.c: Pushing with students aboard wastes critical time.
distractorExplanations.d: Evacuation is required when a train approaches.

prompt: When unloading on the side of the road, students should move:

options.text: Immediately into traffic lanes to hurry home
To a safe location away from traffic as directed, remaining visible to the driver as required
Between buses in a blind alley without guidance
Under the bus to stay warm

explanation: Safe waiting/walking paths keep students out of traffic and wheel areas.

distractorExplanations.c: Blind areas between vehicles are risk zones.

prompt: Counting students during loading/unloading helps ensure:

options.text: Faster driving speeds
Skipping mirror checks
All students are accounted for and none are left around the danger zone unseen
Ignoring the stop arm

explanation: Accountability checks prevent leaving students behind or pulling away while someone is near wheels.

prompt: You must not proceed after loading until:

options.text: A student is still hanging on the door
The stop arm is still required to be out but you cancel early to save time
You guess the mirrors are fine without looking
Students are seated/secure as required and the danger zone is clear

explanation: Movement begins only when boarding is complete and pathways are clear.

distractorExplanations.a: Hanging on doors is extremely dangerous.

prompt: Amber lights (where used in the 8-light system) typically mean:

options.text: The bus is preparing to stop to load/unload students
The bus is broken down on the shoulder only
The driver is turning into a driveway always
Police are requesting a stop of the bus

explanation: Amber warning lights alert traffic before reds/stop arm engage at the stop.

distractorExplanations.b: Ambers are part of student-stop warning sequence.
distractorExplanations.c: Turn signals differ from student ambers.

prompt: If traffic does not stop for your reds/stop arm as required, you should:

options.text: Order students to run across anyway
Keep students from crossing until traffic is safe; report violators per policy
Chase the violator in the bus
Turn off lights and pretend it did not happen without protecting students

explanation: Student safety overrides schedule; do not send children into moving traffic.

distractorExplanations.c: Pursuit is not the driver's role.

prompt: Backing a school bus is:

options.text: Preferred instead of turning around safely
Done at high speed to finish faster
Avoided when possible; if required, use a responsible adult lookout when available and extreme caution
Done while students stand in the danger zone behind the bus

explanation: Backing has huge blind areas; minimize it and use spotters when possible.

distractorExplanations.a: Forward maneuvers are safer when feasible.
distractorExplanations.d: Students behind a backing bus are in grave danger.

prompt: At a multi-lane road stop, students who must cross should:

options.text: Cross behind the bus between stopped cars
Cross without stopping for the driver's signal
Cross diagonally behind trailers out of sight
Cross only after traffic has stopped and the driver gives the crossing signal, walking far enough in front to stay in view

explanation: Front crossing in view is the standard; rear/out-of-sight crossing is forbidden.

distractorExplanations.a: Behind-the-bus crossing is a classic hazard.

prompt: When stopped at railroad tracks, the driver should:

options.text: Open the door/window as required to look and listen for trains
Play loud music to mask train horns
Rely only on a student lookout
Close eyes and count to three

explanation: Sensory checks for trains are part of school-bus RR procedure.

distractorExplanations.c: Driver responsibility cannot be delegated to students.

prompt: Loading on the opposite side of the roadway requiring a student cross is:

options.text: Safer than same-side loading always
Especially hazardous—use proper signals and ensure all traffic is stopped before students enter the roadway
A reason to skip stop-arm use
Allowed while the bus is still rolling

explanation: Crossing students face more traffic conflict; procedures must be deliberate and complete.

distractorExplanations.d: Students never board a rolling bus.

prompt: After students unload at school, before pulling away you should:

options.text: Leave immediately without mirror checks
Assume the parking lot is empty always
Check mirrors and the danger zone carefully for late students and pedestrians
Rely on horn blasts instead of looking

explanation: School loops are crowded; mirror/danger-zone checks prevent strike incidents.

distractorExplanations.a: Immediate departure without checks is unsafe.

prompt: The driver's first responsibility in an emergency is:

options.text: Protect students—stop safely, assess, and follow evacuation/emergency procedures as needed
Save personal belongings first
Argue with bystanders before securing the scene
Leave students alone to find help far away without plan

explanation: Life safety and orderly emergency action come before property or debate.

distractorExplanations.d: Abandonment without a plan endangers students.

prompt: If two students are fighting on the bus, you should:

options.text: Keep driving at highway speed while they fight
Stop the bus safely when needed, separate them using district-approved methods, and report the incident
Join the fight
Ignore serious injury risk

explanation: Safe stop and controlled intervention protect everyone; document and report.

distractorExplanations.a: Driving during a fight is unsafe.

prompt: Evacuation drills teach students to:

options.text: Grab all belongings before moving during a fire
Block aisles to wait for friends
Leave quickly in an orderly way using designated exits without pushing
Exit only through the windshield always

explanation: Orderly evacuation practice saves lives when seconds matter.

distractorExplanations.d: Multiple exits may be used depending on the emergency.

prompt: When a student has a medical emergency on board, the driver should:

options.text: Continue the route without stopping
Give prescription drugs from another student
Post the student's condition on social media
Secure the bus, request emergency help per policy, and provide appropriate first aid within training

explanation: Stop, communicate, and aid within training/policy; privacy still matters.

prompt: Weapons or suspected weapons on the bus require:

options.text: Following district emergency protocols—do not attempt heroics that escalate danger
Physically wrestling for a firearm as the only option always
Ignoring the situation
Allowing students to handle the weapon to 'keep it safe'

explanation: Trained protocol, communication, and law enforcement involvement take priority.

prompt: Good student management starts with:

options.text: Threats and humiliation as primary tools
Clear rules, consistent enforcement, and respectful communication
No rules posted or explained
Favoritism that ignores safety rules for athletes

explanation: Predictable, respectful discipline prevents chaos better than fear-based methods.

distractorExplanations.d: Safety rules apply to all students.

prompt: If a student refuses to stay seated and creates a hazard, you should:

options.text: Speed up so they fall into the seat
Physically punish the student
Address it using approved progressive discipline; stop if safety requires
Let them stand in the stairwell at highway speeds

explanation: Safety interventions follow policy; never use dangerous driving or illegal punishment.

distractorExplanations.a: Using vehicle motion as punishment is abusive/unsafe.
distractorExplanations.d: Stairwell standing underway is extremely dangerous.

prompt: During a tornado threat while en route, guidance often includes:

options.text: Parking on a bridge for a better view
Continuing at top speed through warnings without a plan
Having students film the funnel up close outside
Seeking appropriate shelter per emergency plans rather than staying exposed on the road if directed

explanation: Severe-weather procedures prioritize sturdy shelter and district emergency directions.

prompt: Bus evacuation is usually warranted when:

options.text: Fire, fuel leak, stalling in a dangerous location, or other conditions make staying aboard unsafe
Students are slightly bored
You are one minute late
The radio plays a song you dislike

explanation: Evacuate for true hazards; unnecessary roadside evacuations can create other risks.

distractorExplanations.b: Boredom is not an evacuation trigger.

prompt: Assigning younger students toward the front can help:

options.text: Hide misbehavior from the driver
Improve supervision and reduce unsafe behavior in harder-to-see areas
Encourage standing in the back exclusively
Block the emergency exit with backpacks permanently

explanation: Seating strategies support visibility and control, especially with young riders.

distractorExplanations.a: Goal is visibility, not hiding issues.

prompt: If you suspect a student is being bullied on the bus, you should:

options.text: Encourage the bullying as toughness training
Ignore repeated harassment
Intervene appropriately, document, and report per school policy
Post videos of the bullying online

explanation: Drivers are part of the school's safe climate; bullying requires action and reporting.

distractorExplanations.d: Public posting can retraumatize and violate policy.

prompt: In a hostile parent confrontation at the bus door, prioritize:

options.text: Arguing while students stand in traffic
Allowing the parent to board and threaten students
Driving away with a parent hanging on the door
Student safety, calm communication, and involving school administrators/law enforcement as needed

explanation: Do not let conflicts spill into traffic danger; use backup resources.

distractorExplanations.a: Traffic exposure during arguments is unsafe.
distractorExplanations.b: Unauthorized threatening boarding is not allowed.
distractorExplanations.c: Moving with someone on the door is extremely dangerous.

prompt: Emergency contact/route sheets should be:

options.text: Current and available so you can communicate during incidents
Left at home intentionally
Shared publicly on social media with student addresses
Thrown away each morning

explanation: Accurate operational info supports emergency response while protecting student privacy.

prompt: If smoke appears from the engine area while students are aboard, you should:

options.text: Keep driving to the next town 20 miles away
Stop in a safe place, shut down as appropriate, evacuate, and move students upwind to a safe distance
Tell students to stay seated in thick smoke
Open the fuel door to 'check'

explanation: Smoke/fire risk demands evacuation away from the vehicle and traffic hazards.

prompt: Confidential student information (IEPs, medical plans shared with you) should be:

options.text: Discussed gossip-style with other parents
Posted on a public route blog
Used only as needed for safe transport and kept private
Used to mock the student

explanation: Drivers may need need-to-know medical/behavior info; confidentiality still applies.

prompt: School bus drivers are generally prohibited from:

options.text: Operating with students aboard while using a hand-held phone for non-emergency chatting
Checking mirrors
Using required warning lights
Reporting hazards to dispatch

explanation: Distracted driving rules are strict for passenger-carrying school buses; emergencies have narrow exceptions per law/policy.

prompt: Alcohol and drug use before driving a school bus is:

options.text: Allowed in small amounts if students do not notice
Strictly prohibited—school bus drivers are held to high safety standards
Required for calm nerves
Only banned on field trips

explanation: Zero-tolerance expectations protect children; impaired driving is illegal and disqualifying.

distractorExplanations.d: Bans apply to all student transport duty.

prompt: Leaving students unattended on the bus is generally:

options.text: Encouraged during lunch breaks downtown
Fine if the keys are left in the bus
Prohibited—drivers must supervise or follow approved handoff procedures
Allowed if the radio is on

explanation: Unattended student situations create abduction, injury, and runaway risks.

prompt: Exceeding posted speed limits in a school zone because you are late is:

options.text: Required by CDL rules
Allowed with hazard lights only
Acceptable if no police are visible
Illegal and unsafe—lateness never justifies speeding

explanation: Speed laws protect children; schedule pressure is not a defense.

distractorExplanations.a: CDL rules do not require speeding.
distractorExplanations.c: Enforcement visibility is irrelevant to legality.

prompt: Railroad crossing stop exemptions for school buses:

options.text: Are limited; know state rules—do not assume you may blow crossings
Always allow school buses to ignore all tracks
Mean students should flag trains
Replace the need to look and listen when a stop is required

explanation: Most school buses must stop at tracks unless a specific exemption applies; still use caution.

prompt: Falsifying a pre-trip inspection report is:

options.text: A best practice when late
A serious violation that can lead to termination and legal consequences
Required weekly
Harmless because paperwork does not matter

explanation: Inspection honesty is a safety and compliance obligation.

prompt: Carrying unauthorized adult passengers (not assigned) on a student route is generally:

options.text: Always required
A way to make extra money for the driver
Against policy/rules unless specifically authorized
Allowed if they sit on the steps

explanation: Rider authorization protects students and liability boundaries.

distractorExplanations.d: Steps are not seats and block egress.

prompt: Standees in the stairwell or aisle while the bus is moving are:

options.text: Recommended for overflow
Safe if holding a friend
Allowed above 50 mph only
Prohibited—students must be properly seated as required

explanation: Standing in aisles/stairwells creates ejection and trampling hazards in sudden stops.

distractorExplanations.a: Overflow needs operational solutions, not standing.
distractorExplanations.c: Higher speeds make standing more dangerous.

prompt: Passing a stopped school bus with red lights/stop arm activated (from directions required to stop) is:

options.text: Illegal for other motorists and extremely dangerous to students
Encouraged during rush hour
Legal if the pass is on the right shoulder always
Only a problem in other states, never yours

explanation: Stop-arm violations are a leading student fatality risk; drivers should report them.

distractorExplanations.b: Rush hour does not legalize passing.
distractorExplanations.c: Shoulder passes can still be illegal/deadly.
distractorExplanations.d: Laws apply in your state too.

prompt: Driver hours and fatigue rules matter because:

options.text: Fatigue improves focus
Fatigue impairs reaction time and increases crash risk with children aboard
Sleep is optional for passenger endorsements
Only freight drivers can get tired

explanation: Rested drivers are a core safety control for student transportation.

distractorExplanations.c: Rest is required for safe operation.

prompt: Using the bus for personal errands with students aboard is:

options.text: A fringe benefit
Allowed if you buy students snacks as cover
Prohibited—routes and vehicle use must follow authorized purposes
Required once per week

explanation: Misuse of a school bus violates policy and can endanger students/schedule integrity.

prompt: If your CDL is suspended, you must:

options.text: Continue driving until someone notices
Drive only empty buses on public roads without a CDL when a CDL is required
Lend your license card to another driver
Not drive a school bus—report and follow reinstatement rules

explanation: Operating without required driving privileges is illegal; credential sharing is fraud.

prompt: Idling policies and local rules may require drivers to:

options.text: Minimize unnecessary idling for health/environmental and policy compliance
Idle with doors open in a closed garage for fun
Ignore all posted school idling limits
Rev engines to entertain students

explanation: Many districts limit idling near students due to exhaust exposure.

prompt: Refusing a required drug/alcohol test when ordered under applicable rules can result in:

options.text: A free lunch coupon
Disqualification/removal from safety-sensitive functions
Automatic promotion
No consequences ever

explanation: Testing refusals are typically treated like positive results under FMCSA/employer rules.

prompt: The school bus endorsement (S) generally requires:

options.text: No extra knowledge beyond a bicycle license
Only a written note from a friend
Passing knowledge (and skills as required) tests specific to school bus operation in addition to base CDL qualifications
Owning the bus personally

explanation: S endorsement validates school-bus-specific knowledge/skills beyond general CDL.
